corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

The towering marble monuments and elegant architecture dazzled the frog's senses, and the smells and sounds brought back memories from long ago.

Our prompt dataset consists primarily of text prompts submitted to the OpenAI API, specifically those using an earlier version of the InstructGPT models (trained via supervised learning on a subset of our demonstration data) on the Playground interface.

From these prompts, we produce three different datasets used in our fine-tuning procedure: (1) our SFT dataset, with labeler demonstrations used to train our SFT models, (2) our RM dataset, with labeler rankings of model outputs used to train our RMs, and (3) our PPO dataset, without any human labels, which are used as inputs for RLHF fine-tuning.

The SFT dataset contains about 13k training prompts (from the API and labeler-written), the RM dataset has 33k training prompts (from the API and labeler-written), and the PPO dataset has 31k training prompts (only from the API).
To give a sense of the composition of our dataset, in Table we show the distribution of use-case categories for our API prompts (specifically the RM dataset) as labeled by our contractors.

Instead, we measure truthfulness-whether the model's statements about the world are true-using two metrics: (1) evaluating our model's tendency to make up information on closed domain tasks ("hallucinations"), and (2) using the TruthfulQA dataset .
We also benchmark our model on datasets intended to measure bias and toxicity, such as RealToxicityPrompts and CrowS-Pairs .
We also conduct human evaluations of toxicity on the RealToxicityPrompts dataset .

We also found that our results do not change significantly when evaluated on prompts submitted to GPT-3 models on the API (see Figure ), though our PPO-ptx models perform slightly worse at larger model sizes.
Note that, due to dataset sizes, these results are collapsed across model sizes.

These RMs have an accuracy of 69.6 ± 0.9% on predicting the preferences of labelers in the held-out group, a small decrease from their 72.4 ± 0.4% accuracy on predicting the preferences of labelers in their training set.

However, classification and QA are only a small part (about 18%) of what API customers use our language models for, whereas open-ended generation and brainstorming consist of about 57% of our prompt dataset according to labelers (see Table ).
As measured by human evaluatoins on the TruthfulQA dataset, our PPO models show small but significant improvements in generating truthful and informative outputs compared to GPT-3 (see Figure ).
As measured by human evaluatoins on the TruthfulQA dataset, our PPO models show small but significant improvements in generating truthful and informative outputs compared to GPT-3 (see Figure ).

We perform automatic evaluations on the following benchmark datasets: Winogender , CrowS-Pairs , RealToxicityPrompts , TruthfulQA , DROP , QuAC , SquadV2 , Hellaswag , SST , RTE and WSC (both part of Super-GLUE ), WMT 15 Fr → En , CNN/Daily Mail Summarization , and Reddit TLDR Summarization .
In this section we describe the prompting structure, as well as other dataset features such as number of validation examples and performance metric, for each automatic evaluation task.
